quoteFirst: The Queen and Prince were more than ever impressed that the strength of Cherbourg was a menace to England, and called the attention of their own Ministers, who were in attendance, to the obvious necessity for England to look more sharply to her coast and naval defences. How thankful Her Majesty must have been when the end of each day’s festivity was reached! Even in the diary the mere words form a little oasis, “At
quoteLast: we went below, and read and nearly finished that most interesting book, ‘Jane Eyre.’”
title: Life of Her Majesty Queen Victoria
author: Fawcett, Millicent Garrett, Dame, 1847-1929

quoteFirst: Why had he spoken of Viola? And then, in the flight of fancies which surged through his mind, there was one that he stayed and detained. It was that he must see her again before she left town. He looked at his watch: it lacked
quoteLast: , and on the impulse of the moment he hailed a passing 'bus. It was inexplicable to him that the night before she should have let him go without a word as to her movements.
title: The Truth About Tristrem Varick: A Novel
author: Saltus, Edgar, 1855-1921

quoteFirst: The notion was that our 18-pounders and 4.5 hows. with a couple of trench-mortars, would open heavily at
quoteLast: . Ten minutes later, the Stokes mortars were to join in. At ten the guns would lift and make a barrage while the Stokes mortars attended to the flanks of the attack.
title: The Irish Guards in the Great War, Volume 2 (of 2)
author: Kipling, Rudyard, 1865-1936

quoteLast: , we reached Marseilles at nearly midnight, feeling very tired, and were glad to get to the Terminus Hotel, which is comfortably close to the station. What a charming station it is, with its courtyard and garden, orange trees and flowering myrtles!
title:
Fair Italy, the Riviera and Monte Carlo
Comprising a Tour Through North and South Italy and Sicily with a Short Account of Malta
author: Devereux, W. Cope (William Cope), 1837-1903

quoteFirst: They spun back to Wissining in something close to ten minutes, stopping, with a triumphant toot of the horn, in front of Clarke at
quoteLast: . Half a dozen fellows who had seen the arrival of the automobile rushed up to shake Dan’s hand and inquire anxiously if he were going to play.
title: Change Signals: A Story of the New Football
author: Barbour, Ralph Henry, 1870-1944; Relyea, C. M. (Charles M.), 1863-1932 [Illustrator]

quoteFirst: At eight o'clock Captain Sigsbee received the reports from the different officers of the ship that every thing was secure for the night. At ten minutes after nine the bugler sounded "taps," the signal for "turning in," and soon the ship was quiet. At
quoteLast: a sharp explosion was heard, then a loud, long, roaring sound, mingled with the noise of falling timbers; the electric lights went out, the ship was lifted up, and then she began to sink. The Captain and some of the other officers groped their way to the deck, hardly knowing what had happened.
title: Young Peoples' History of the War with Spain
author: Holmes, Prescott

quoteFirst: "I don't know exactly, but Nita said she'd had to dash away at an ungodly hour, so that Lydia could make her ten o'clock dentist's appointment, and so that she herself could get a manicure and a shampoo and have her hair dressed, so I imagine she must have left not later than fifteen or
title: Murder at Bridge
author: Austin, Anne, 1895-1975

quoteFirst: The sun shone brightly, the sky was clear, the sea was calm, and a breeze blew lightly from the north-west. It was one of the rare bright stretches that visit the Islands, for usually rain falls, mostly in misty drizzles, on about 250 days in the year. At
quoteLast: the _Glasgow_ weighed anchor, and joined the _Kent_ at the harbour-mouth. Five minutes later the rest of the squadron weighed, and began to steam out.
title: World's War Events, Vol. I
author: Reynolds, Francis J. (Francis Joseph), 1867-1937 [Editor]; Churchill, Allen L. (Allen Leon), 1873-1940 [Editor]

quoteFirst: 30 resumed our journey to the south-south-west, and reached the head of the creek at 8.0 a.m. Ascending the tableland by an abrupt slope of 100 feet, our course was south one mile, when the southern slope was reached, and a large shallow valley extended across our course, beyond which a vast and slightly undulating plain extended to the horizon with scarcely a rising ground to relieve its extreme monotony. Descending by a very gentle slope into the valley, at
quoteLast: crossed a small watercourse trending south-east, and then passed through a plain densely covered with kangaroo-grass seven to nine feet high, and at 10.40 a.m. encountered the level sandy country beyond, which was covered with triodia and small acacia and gum trees, or rather bushes.
title: Journals of Australian Explorations
author: Gregory, Augustus Charles, 1819-1905; Gregory, Francis Thomas, 1821-1888
